text: We showed that temperature-gradient mass transfer occurred in a Hastelloy N thermal convection loop circulating a sodium fluoroborate-sodium fluoride mixture $(\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole $\%$ NaF) at temperatures of $687^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ maximum and $438^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ minimum for 19,930 hr.
The maximum calculated corrosion rate (based on uniform removal) was 0.29 mil/year.
A thermal convection loop, designated NCL-20 and similar to that pictured in the foreground of Fig.
1, began operation in December 1969 and was drained in March 1972, after 19,930 hr at design conditions.

The purpose of the test was to determine the compatibility of standard Hastelloy N with a sodium fluoroborate-sodium fluoride eutectic mixture, $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole $\%$ NaF, at the most extreme temperature conditions considered (687°C maximum and 438°C minimum) for the molten-salt breeder reactor (MSBR) secondary circuit.

The corrosion of Hastelloy N by the sodium fluoroborate mixture has been studied for several years in both thermal convection and pumped loops but usually at temperatures of $610^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and lower. $^{1-13}$ The experiment discussed in this report was conducted to determine the mass transfer characteristics at higher temperatures with a larger temperature gradient.
Flow was generated by the difference in density of the salt in the hot and cold legs of the loop, and the salt flow velocity was approximately 7 ft/min.
Litman, Compatibility of Hastelloy $N$ and Croloy 9M with $NaBF_4 \cdot NaF \cdot KBF_4$ (90-4-6 Mole %) Fluoroborate Salt, ORNL-TM-2490 (April 1969).
Litman, Catastrophic Corrosion of Type 304 Stainless Steel in a System Circulating Fused Sodium Fluoroborate, ORNL-TM-2741 (January 1970).
Litman, Compatibility of Fused Sodium Fluoroborates and $BF_{3}$ Gas with Hastelloy N Alloys, ORNL-TM-2978 (June 1970).
Koger, Corrosion and Mass Transfer Characteristics of $\mathsf{NaBF}_4$ -NaF (92-8 Mole %) in Hastelloy N, ORNL-TM-3866 (October 1972).

Hastelloy N thermal convection loop NCL-20 containing $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole $\%$ NaF at a maximum temperature of $687^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with a temperature difference of $249^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
at a maximum temperature of $687^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a temperature difference of $249^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The harp portion of the loop system was fabricated from 0.750-in.
OD, 0.072-in. wall Hastelloy N tubing.
This material was part of heat 5095, which was purchased from Wall Tube and Metal Products Company, Newport, Tennessee.
The Oak Ridge National Laboratory Inspection Engineering identification number is IR-8402-2.
The finished loop system was stress relieved at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 6 hr in hydrogen.

The loop contained 14 standard Hastelloy N specimens and two titanium-modified Hastelloy N specimens $0.75 \times 0.38 \times 0.030$ in., each with a surface area of 0.55 in. $^{2}$ ( $3.5\mathrm{cm}^{2}$ ).
The compositions of the standard and modified Hastelloy N specimens are given in Table 1.
Eight specimens were attached at different vertical positions on each of two $\frac{1}{8}$ -in. rods.
The fluoroborate salt mixture was furnished by the Fluoride Processing Group of the Reactor Chemistry Division, and its composition before test is given in Table 2.
To mix and purify the salt, the raw materials were first heated in a nickel-lined vessel to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under vacuum and held for $15\mathrm{hr}$ .
Then the salt was heated to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , agitated with helium for a few hours, and transferred to the fill vessel.

Before filling with salt, the loop was degreased with ethyl alcohol, dried, and then heated to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under vacuum to remove any traces of moisture.

The loop was filled by heating the harp section, the salt pot, and all connecting lines to approximately $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and applying helium pressure to the salt supply vessel to force the salt into the system.
A flush salt charge, intended to remove surface oxides and other impurities, was held for $24\mathrm{hr}$ in the loop at the maximum operation temperature and then dumped.
Helium cover gas of $99.998\%$ purity and at a slight positive pressure (approx 5 psig) was maintained over the salt during operation.

4 and the various micrographs, we detected material loss in the hot portions of the loop, material deposition in the cold portion, and a point of little change (balance point) at about 580 or $590^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
If iron and chromium had been completely removed, the remaining alloy would have contained $80.5\%$ Ni and $19.5\%$ Mo.
Analysis showed $71\%$ Ni and $29\%$ Mo; thus some nickel may also have been removed.
\mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N a B F} _ {4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {N a B F} _ {3} \mathrm {O H} + \mathrm {H F}. \tag {1}
6 \mathrm {H F} + 2 \mathrm {C r} + 6 \mathrm {N a F} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm {N a} _ {3} \mathrm {C r F} _ {6} + 3 \mathrm {H} _ {2}, \tag {2a}

A previous Hastelloy N loop that circulated equimolar $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4}$ salt saturated with HF operated only $200\mathrm{hr}$ before it became plugged with nickel corrosion product. $^{17}$ Attack of the Hastelloy N in this case was uniform throughout the loop.
\mathrm {N a B F} _ {3} \mathrm {O H} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {N a B F} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {H F}. \tag {3}

6 \mathrm {N a F} + 3 \mathrm {N a B F} _ {4} + 3 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + 2 \mathrm {C r} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm {N a B F} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + 2 \mathrm {N a} _ {3} \mathrm {C r F} _ {6} + 3 \mathrm {H} _ {2}. \tag {4}

6 \mathrm {N a F} + 6 \mathrm {N a B F} _ {3} \mathrm {O H} + 2 \mathrm {C r} \rightleftharpoons 6 \mathrm {N a B F} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + 2 \mathrm {N a} _ {3} \mathrm {C r F} _ {6} + 3 \mathrm {H} _ {2}. \tag {5}
For a solid-state diffusion-controlled process, $b = \frac{1}{2}$ , and for a dissolution-controlled process $b = 1$ (ref.
Calculations showed that for the first $11,900 \, \text{hr} \, a = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ and $b = 1.1$ .
The almost straight-line dependence of weight loss with time ( $b = 1.1$ ) also points to a fairly general type of attack, as opposed to selective removal of only one element.
The salt analysis showed evidence of reaction (1) in that an ionic species of iron was removed from the salt while the concentration of chromium ions increased.

Voids of this same type have also been developed in Inconel by high-temperature oxidation tests and high-temperature vacuum tests in which chromium is selectively removed.[19] Voids similar to these have also been developed in copper-brass diffusion couples and by thedezincification of brass.[20] All of these phenomena arise from the so-called Kirkendall effect; that is, solute atoms of a given type diffuse out at a faster rate than other atoms comprising the crystal lattice can diffuse in to fill vacancies that result from the outward diffusion.

Temperature-gradient mass transfer of Hastelloy N, exposed to a sodium fluoroborate mixture of $\mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8$ mole $\%$ NaF in a thermal convection loop system that operated at temperatures of $687^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ maximum and $438^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ minimum for $19,930\mathrm{hr}$ , does occur.
The maximum calculated corrosion rate (based on uniform removal) was $0.29\mathrm{mil/year}$ .
